state.planMarkdown:
Bot: CryptoIntel Bot Production Plan for Telegram

Summary:
CryptoIntel Bot is a Telegram-based crypto intelligence assistant built with Node.js ESM and grammY. It gives users fast, structured, source-cited analysis for token security, market prices, technical signals, NFT collections, wallets, news, and sentiment.

The production design prioritizes concise verdict-first responses, strict safety disclaimers, graceful handling of missing data, multilingual English and basic Indonesian support, and reliable operation as a single Node.js service. The bot should not pretend to have certainty where data is incomplete; unknown or newly launched assets are flagged as insufficient data.

Product goals:
The main product goal is to turn fragmented crypto data into quick Telegram-native intelligence. Users should be able to paste a contract address, token symbol, wallet address, or NFT collection name and receive a concise answer with a clear verdict, cited sources, and practical risk warnings.

1) Security first: every token scan must produce SAFE, RISKY, or SCAM, with specific reasons such as honeypot behavior, owner privileges, liquidity risk, holder concentration, blacklist logic, mint capability, and trading restrictions.

2) Market clarity: price responses should combine market data with lightweight technical analysis, including price, 24h change, market cap, volume, RSI, MACD, EMA trend, support, resistance, and a bullish, neutral, or bearish short-term signal.

3) NFT coverage: NFT responses should summarize floor price, volume, holders, listings, notable sales, whale behavior, and collection risk using sources such as OpenSea, Blur, and Magic Eden where available.

4) Wallet insight: wallet responses should identify token balances, approximate USD values, NFT holdings, recent transaction patterns, and possible PnL only when historical data is sufficiently available.

5) News and sentiment: news responses should summarize major headlines, social sentiment direction, trending narratives, and upcoming crypto events while clearly citing sources and timestamps.

6) Safety and compliance posture: the bot must never provide financial advice. Every trading-related response should include a short disclaimer and should warn users against phishing links, fake airdrops, seed phrase requests, and impersonation scams.

7) Language behavior: default to English, but if a user writes in Indonesian, respond in basic Indonesian while preserving the same structure and risk disclaimers.

High-level architecture:
The bot runs as one Node.js ESM service using grammY for Telegram interaction. The service contains the Telegram adapter, command router, intent detector, analysis orchestrator, provider clients, response formatter, persistence layer, scheduler, and observability utilities in one deployable process.

1) Telegram entry layer: grammY receives updates from Telegram using TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables. It handles commands, free-text messages, callback buttons if used, errors, and per-chat rate limiting. The bot should support both explicit commands and natural inputs, such as a pasted contract address or wallet address.

2) Intent and entity detection: incoming messages are normalized, language-detected, and classified as scan, price, NFT, wallet, news, sentiment, help, or unknown. Address detection should distinguish probable EVM addresses, Solana addresses, and ambiguous strings. Token symbols and NFT collection names should go through resolution before analysis.

3) Analysis orchestrator: each command calls a dedicated domain service. Token scan service aggregates contract, liquidity, trading, holder, and market data. Price service aggregates spot market data and technical indicators. NFT service aggregates collection data. Wallet service aggregates balances, NFTs, and recent activity. News service aggregates headlines and trends. Sentiment service aggregates social and narrative signals where available.

4) Provider abstraction: all external data sources are hidden behind provider interfaces so the bot can combine multiple sources and degrade gracefully if one fails. Target sources include DEXScreener, CoinGecko, DefiLlama, chain explorers or RPC providers, OpenSea, Blur, Magic Eden, and reputable crypto news or events feeds. Every final answer must cite the sources actually used.

5) Risk scoring engine: token scans use a deterministic scoring model. Critical flags such as honeypot behavior, blocked selling, unlocked liquidity with low depth, owner mint privileges, blacklist controls, or extreme holder concentration can immediately push the verdict to SCAM or RISKY. Lower severity issues contribute to a cumulative risk score. The final verdict should always include the top reasons.

6) Technical analysis engine: price analysis should calculate RSI, MACD, EMA trend, and simple support and resistance from recent candle data. If candle history is too short or stale, the bot should say technical indicators are unavailable instead of inventing values.

7) Response formatter: all command responses must be under 300 words, verdict-first, and use the requested emoji sections: 🔍 SECURITY, 📊 PRICE, 🖼️ NFT, 💼 PORTFOLIO, 📰 NEWS, and 🎯 SENTIMENT. Responses should include data timestamp, sources, uncertainty notes, and a short non-financial-advice disclaimer.

8) Persistence and caching: MongoDB stores users, preferences, request logs, cached asset lookups, scan snapshots, wallet summaries, and provider health metadata. Simple in-memory maps can be used for short-lived deduplication and hot caches, but MongoDB is preferred for production continuity.

9) Background tasks: an in-process scheduler refreshes popular token metadata, provider health, narrative trends, and news caches. Since the system is a single process, scheduled jobs must be lightweight, idempotent, and safe to skip if the service restarts.

10) Failure behavior: if data is unavailable, stale, contradictory, or rate-limited, the bot clearly states the limitation, cites what was reachable, and marks the result as insufficient data when appropriate.

Data model:
MongoDB should be used for durable production persistence. In-memory maps can support short-lived rate limits, response deduplication, and temporary provider cache, but important operational and product data should live in MongoDB.

1) users: stores Telegram user id, chat id, username if available, preferred language, first seen time, last seen time, command usage counts, and safety flags such as muted or blocked.

2) requests: stores command type, normalized input, detected chain, user id reference, timestamp, latency, response status, provider sources used, error category if any, and whether the final result was complete or partial.

3) assets: stores normalized token identity such as chain, contract address, symbol, name, decimals, logo reference if available, source confidence, first discovered time, and last refreshed time.

4) tokenscansnapshots: stores chain, contract address, verdict, numeric risk score, risk factors, holder summary, liquidity summary, owner and permission flags, honeypot result, source list, block or timestamp, and expiry time.

5) marketsnapshots: stores asset id, price, 24h change, market cap, volume, liquidity, RSI, MACD state, EMA trend, support, resistance, signal, source list, candle window, and timestamp.

6) nftcollections: stores collection name, slug or identifier, chain, marketplace identifiers, floor price, volume windows, holder count, listing count, notable sales summary, risk notes, source list, and last refreshed time.

7) walletsnapshots: stores wallet address, chain, token holdings summary, NFT holdings summary, estimated USD value, recent transaction pattern tags, approximate PnL availability, source list, and timestamp. For privacy, avoid storing unnecessary raw transaction details longer than needed.

8) newsitems: stores headline, summary, source, URL if allowed by product policy, category, detected narrative, sentiment label, published time, and ingestion time.

9) providerhealth: stores provider name, last success time, last failure time, failure count, average latency, and current degraded status.

10) settings: stores global runtime configuration such as enabled chains, cache TTL values, feature flags, response word limit, and supported language defaults.

Key flows:
1) Startup flow: the service reads TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and required provider credentials from environment variables, connects to MongoDB if enabled, initializes grammY middleware, loads settings, warms minimal caches, registers commands, starts the Telegram update handling mode, and starts lightweight scheduled refresh tasks.

2) Scan flow: user sends /scan contractaddress or pastes a contract address. The bot detects the chain if possible, resolves token metadata, fetches contract flags, ownership status, mint and blacklist functions, liquidity depth, lock status where available, honeypot or sellability signals, holder concentration, and recent volume. The risk engine assigns SAFE, RISKY, or SCAM. The formatter returns 🔍 SCAN RESULTS with token, chain, verdict, key findings, sources, timestamp, and disclaimer.

3) Price flow: user sends /price tokensymbol. The bot resolves the symbol across supported chains and asks for clarification if ambiguous. It fetches price, 24h change, market cap, volume, liquidity, and recent candles. It calculates RSI, MACD, EMA trend, support, and resistance when enough data exists. It returns 📊 PRICE with a bullish, neutral, or bearish signal and source citations.

4) NFT flow: user sends /nft collectionname. The bot resolves the collection across OpenSea, Blur, Magic Eden, and known chain identifiers. It fetches floor price, 24h and 7d trends, volume, holders, listings, notable sales, whale activity if available, and risk indicators such as wash trading suspicion, low liquidity, suspicious metadata changes, or holder concentration. It returns 🖼️ NFT with concise findings.

5) Wallet flow: user sends /wallet address. The bot identifies chain compatibility, fetches token balances, NFT holdings, recent transactions, and current USD values. It estimates PnL only if historical cost basis or sufficient transaction history is available. It returns 💼 PORTFOLIO and avoids exposing sensitive assumptions as facts.

6) News flow: user sends /news. The bot pulls the latest cached headlines and trend summaries, groups them by major narratives such as AI, DeFi, Gaming, RWA, Memecoins, and infrastructure, then returns 📰 NEWS with sources and timestamps.

7) Sentiment flow: user sends /sentiment token. The bot resolves the token, aggregates available social and market sentiment signals, classifies bullish, bearish, or neutral, and explains the drivers. If social data is sparse, it returns insufficient data rather than overclaiming.

8) Free-text flow: if the user asks naturally, such as is this CA safe or apa kabar ETH hari ini, the bot detects intent and routes to the closest command. If confidence is low, it asks a short clarification question.

9) Error flow: provider timeout, missing data, ambiguous symbol, unsupported chain, or invalid address triggers a helpful response. The bot should not expose internal stack traces. It should say what failed, what sources were checked, and what the user can try next.

10) Safety flow: any message containing seed phrases, private keys, or phishing-like content should trigger a warning. The bot should tell users never to share seed phrases or private keys and should not process or store sensitive secret material.

Tech stack:
The production stack is intentionally simple and aligned with a single-process Node.js deployment.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ECMAScript modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY. The bot token is read from TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN in environment variables.

3) Persistence: MongoDB for users, requests, cached data, scan snapshots, wallet summaries, news items, and provider health. Simple in-memory maps may be used for short-lived rate limiting, deduplication, and hot cache entries.

4) External data: provider clients for DEXScreener, CoinGecko, DefiLlama, chain explorers or RPC endpoints, OpenSea, Blur, Magic Eden, and reputable crypto news, events, and sentiment sources. Provider selection should be configurable so unavailable or degraded sources can be bypassed.

5) File storage: object storage or simple filesystem storage only if needed for generated reports, cached images, or exported diagnostics. The core bot can operate without file storage.

6) Scheduling: lightweight in-process scheduled tasks for refreshing popular assets, news, provider health, and expiring stale cache entries.

7) Deployment: one Node.js server in the cloud running a single service process. Configuration is supplied by environment variables. No additional databases, message brokers, or separate infrastructure components are required.

8) Observability: structured application logs, request latency tracking, provider health metrics, command usage summaries, and error categorization inside the same service and MongoDB where useful.

9) Security: environment-based secrets, strict input validation, outbound request timeouts, provider response validation, no storage of private keys or seed phrases, and conservative response behavior when data is incomplete.

10) Testing strategy: unit tests for parsing, risk scoring, technical indicators, formatting, and language switching; integration tests with mocked provider responses; and manual Telegram smoke tests before production release.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Reliability: the bot should degrade gracefully when data providers fail. A partial response with clear source limitations is better than a fabricated complete answer. Provider calls should use timeouts, retries with limits, and fallback ordering.

2) Latency: common commands should target a fast response by using cached metadata and parallel provider calls. If a full scan takes longer, the bot can send a short processing message and then edit or follow up with results.

3) Accuracy: every factual market or security response must include source names and data timestamps. Technical indicators must only be shown when enough recent candle data is available.

4) Rate limiting: per-user and per-chat limits should protect the single process from spam. Heavy commands like /scan and /wallet should have stricter limits than /help or /about.

5) Scalability within constraints: optimize for a single process by caching popular assets, limiting concurrent provider calls, avoiding expensive background jobs, and keeping responses concise.

6) Privacy: store only what is needed for product quality and abuse prevention. Do not store seed phrases, private keys, or sensitive user secrets. Wallet addresses are public-chain data but should still be treated carefully in logs and retention policy.

7) Safety: the bot must never present output as financial advice. It should consistently remind users to do their own research and avoid phishing, fake airdrops, seed phrase requests, malicious token approvals, and impersonator links.

8) Internationalization: language detection should be lightweight. English is default. Indonesian responses should use simple, clear wording and preserve crypto terms where common.

9) Maintainability: keep domain services separated by capability, keep provider integrations behind stable interfaces, and keep response templates centralized so command formatting remains consistent.

10) Abuse handling: detect repeated invalid inputs, suspicious link spam, and attempts to make the bot process secrets. Apply cooldowns, warnings, or silent rejection as appropriate.

Bot commands:
• /start — Introduces CryptoIntel Bot, explains supported inputs, sets the default language behavior, and warns users not to share seed phrases or private keys. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Shows all available commands, supported chains, response rules, and examples for contract addresses, token symbols, wallets, NFT collections, news, and sentiment. (usage: /help) [general]
• /about — Explains the bot capabilities, supported data categories, source-citation policy, limitations, and non-financial-advice disclaimer. (usage: /about) [general]
• /scan — Runs a full token security audit and returns a SAFE, RISKY, or SCAM verdict with specific reasons, sources, and timestamp. (usage: /scan 0xcontractaddress) [security]
• /price — Returns real-time market data and technical analysis for a token, including price, 24h change, market cap, volume, RSI, MACD, EMA trend, support, resistance, and signal. (usage: /price ETH) [market]
• /nft — Returns NFT collection intelligence, including floor price, 24h and 7d trends, volume, holders, listings, notable sales, whale activity, and risk notes. (usage: /nft Pudgy Penguins) [nft]
• /wallet — Analyzes a public wallet address and summarizes token holdings, current USD value, NFT overview, recent transaction patterns, and PnL only when enough historical data exists. (usage: /wallet 0xwalletaddress) [portfolio]
• /news — Shows concise latest crypto headlines, trending narratives, major upcoming events, and source citations. (usage: /news) [news]
• /sentiment — Summarizes bullish, bearish, or neutral sentiment for a token or narrative using available social, news, and market signals. (usage: /sentiment SOL) [sentiment]

planSections.productGoals:
The main product goal is to turn fragmented crypto data into quick Telegram-native intelligence. Users should be able to paste a contract address, token symbol, wallet address, or NFT collection name and receive a concise answer with a clear verdict, cited sources, and practical risk warnings.

1) Security first: every token scan must produce SAFE, RISKY, or SCAM, with specific reasons such as honeypot behavior, owner privileges, liquidity risk, holder concentration, blacklist logic, mint capability, and trading restrictions.

2) Market clarity: price responses should combine market data with lightweight technical analysis, including price, 24h change, market cap, volume, RSI, MACD, EMA trend, support, resistance, and a bullish, neutral, or bearish short-term signal.

3) NFT coverage: NFT responses should summarize floor price, volume, holders, listings, notable sales, whale behavior, and collection risk using sources such as OpenSea, Blur, and Magic Eden where available.

4) Wallet insight: wallet responses should identify token balances, approximate USD values, NFT holdings, recent transaction patterns, and possible PnL only when historical data is sufficiently available.

5) News and sentiment: news responses should summarize major headlines, social sentiment direction, trending narratives, and upcoming crypto events while clearly citing sources and timestamps.

6) Safety and compliance posture: the bot must never provide financial advice. Every trading-related response should include a short disclaimer and should warn users against phishing links, fake airdrops, seed phrase requests, and impersonation scams.

7) Language behavior: default to English, but if a user writes in Indonesian, respond in basic Indonesian while preserving the same structure and risk disclaimers.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
The bot runs as one Node.js ESM service using grammY for Telegram interaction. The service contains the Telegram adapter, command router, intent detector, analysis orchestrator, provider clients, response formatter, persistence layer, scheduler, and observability utilities in one deployable process.

1) Telegram entry layer: grammY receives updates from Telegram using TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables. It handles commands, free-text messages, callback buttons if used, errors, and per-chat rate limiting. The bot should support both explicit commands and natural inputs, such as a pasted contract address or wallet address.

2) Intent and entity detection: incoming messages are normalized, language-detected, and classified as scan, price, NFT, wallet, news, sentiment, help, or unknown. Address detection should distinguish probable EVM addresses, Solana addresses, and ambiguous strings. Token symbols and NFT collection names should go through resolution before analysis.

3) Analysis orchestrator: each command calls a dedicated domain service. Token scan service aggregates contract, liquidity, trading, holder, and market data. Price service aggregates spot market data and technical indicators. NFT service aggregates collection data. Wallet service aggregates balances, NFTs, and recent activity. News service aggregates headlines and trends. Sentiment service aggregates social and narrative signals where available.

4) Provider abstraction: all external data sources are hidden behind provider interfaces so the bot can combine multiple sources and degrade gracefully if one fails. Target sources include DEXScreener, CoinGecko, DefiLlama, chain explorers or RPC providers, OpenSea, Blur, Magic Eden, and reputable crypto news or events feeds. Every final answer must cite the sources actually used.

5) Risk scoring engine: token scans use a deterministic scoring model. Critical flags such as honeypot behavior, blocked selling, unlocked liquidity with low depth, owner mint privileges, blacklist controls, or extreme holder concentration can immediately push the verdict to SCAM or RISKY. Lower severity issues contribute to a cumulative risk score. The final verdict should always include the top reasons.

6) Technical analysis engine: price analysis should calculate RSI, MACD, EMA trend, and simple support and resistance from recent candle data. If candle history is too short or stale, the bot should say technical indicators are unavailable instead of inventing values.

7) Response formatter: all command responses must be under 300 words, verdict-first, and use the requested emoji sections: 🔍 SECURITY, 📊 PRICE, 🖼️ NFT, 💼 PORTFOLIO, 📰 NEWS, and 🎯 SENTIMENT. Responses should include data timestamp, sources, uncertainty notes, and a short non-financial-advice disclaimer.

8) Persistence and caching: MongoDB stores users, preferences, request logs, cached asset lookups, scan snapshots, wallet summaries, and provider health metadata. Simple in-memory maps can be used for short-lived deduplication and hot caches, but MongoDB is preferred for production continuity.

9) Background tasks: an in-process scheduler refreshes popular token metadata, provider health, narrative trends, and news caches. Since the system is a single process, scheduled jobs must be lightweight, idempotent, and safe to skip if the service restarts.

10) Failure behavior: if data is unavailable, stale, contradictory, or rate-limited, the bot clearly states the limitation, cites what was reachable, and marks the result as insufficient data when appropriate.

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Required token used by grammY to run the Telegram bot.
envVarDocs.usage: Read in src/lib/config.js and validated in src/index.js before polling starts.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with BotFather on Telegram.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Gateway Endpoint
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for AI synthesis and ChainGPT news through CookMyBots.
envVarDocs.usage: src/lib/ai.js appends /chat and /chaingpt/news to this base URL.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Gateway Key
envVarDocs.description: Secret key used in Authorization: Bearer for CookMyBots AI Gateway calls.
envVarDocs.usage: Used only by src/lib/ai.js for AI Gateway authentication.

envVarDocs.title: OpenSea API Key
envVarDocs.description: Optional key for richer OpenSea NFT collection data.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/services/nft.js only when present. NFT features degrade gracefully if missing.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Request an API key from OpenSea.

envVarDocs.title: AI Timeout
envVarDocs.description: Optional timeout for AI Gateway calls.
envVarDocs.usage: Defaults to 600000 milliseconds.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set only if you need to tune long AI requests.

envVarDocs.title: AI Retry Count
envVarDocs.description: Optional retry count for transient AI Gateway failures.
envVarDocs.usage: Defaults to 2.

envVarDocs.title: General Concurrency
envVarDocs.description: Optional concurrency setting reserved for runtime tuning.
envVarDocs.usage: Defaults to 20 in config; Telegram AI runner concurrency remains conservative.

envVarDocs.title: Global AI Cap
envVarDocs.description: Optional cap for simultaneous AI-heavy jobs.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/lib/backpressure.js. Defaults to 1.

envVarDocs.title: External API Timeout
envVarDocs.description: Optional timeout for non-AI external data source calls.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/lib/http.js. Defaults to 20000 milliseconds.
